articles.title: The Plan: Lower Manhattan’s Wrey Rental Tower Is Designed Like a Private Club
articles.narrative: GFP Development is converting a 31-story former office building at 222 Broadway in Lower Manhattan into a 788-unit luxury rental tower named The Wrey. The project, which began move-ins in June and is expected to be fully delivered in Q1 next year, aims to meet significant demand from professionals seeking high-quality housing in the area. The building is designed with extensive amenities, including two pools, a bathhouse, coworking spaces, and a private dining room, to create an exclusive club-like atmosphere for residents. The developer is also considering opting into a state tax break program that requires a percentage of units to be affordable.

data_points.notable_features: Luxury rental tower converted from a former office building, featuring two pools, a bathhouse, coworking space, private dining, and a spa. Located near City Hall Park, overlooking Broadway, Saint Paul's Chapel, and the World Trade Center.
data_points.project_notes: Developer is considering opting into the state's 467-m program, which offers tax breaks for nonresidential-to-housing conversions with an affordable housing component. The building's name is inspired by the Jacob Wrey Mould memorial fountain in City Hall Park.

articles.title: S3 Capital Lends $102M for Hell’s Kitchen Office-to-Resi Conversion
articles.narrative: S3 Capital has provided $102 million in construction financing to Hershy Silberstein for the conversion of the Press Building in Hell's Kitchen, Manhattan, from office to residential use. The project will create 160 apartments and 39,712 square feet of commercial space. This transaction marks S3 Capital's first office-to-residential conversion loan, attracted by the sponsor's strong relationship with the lender, the property's prime location in a supply-constrained submarket, and its suitable layout for conversion. The development will benefit from the 467-m tax abatement program, with 25% of units designated for affordable housing.

data_points.address: 311 West 43rd Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Located west of Times Square between Eighth and Ninth avenues; benefits from the 467-m tax abatement program.
data_points.project_notes: 25% of units will be set aside for people earning an average of 80% of area median income. The 15-story building was previously acquired by DivcoWest Real Estate Investments in December 2018 for $131 million. Hershy Silberstein recently acquired the property for an undisclosed price after a prior deal with David Werner for around $40 million fell through.

articles.market_intelligence: The Midtown West area of Manhattan has delivered 4.5% annual rent growth over the last five years with a vacancy rate of 3.5% in that period.

articles.title: TA Realty Buys Two Staten Island Retail Buildings for $79M
articles.narrative: TA Realty has acquired two retail buildings totaling 184,204 square feet within the Tysens Park Shopping Center in Staten Island, New York, for $79.2 million from the Bregman Organization. The transaction includes significant air rights, providing the new owner with substantial redevelopment potential. This acquisition represents a notable expansion into the retail sector for TA Realty, which is primarily known for its industrial portfolio.

data_points.address: 2712 Hylan Boulevard and 2754 Hylan Boulevard, Staten Island, NY
data_points.notable_features: Includes 632,284 square feet of air rights, offering 818,733 square feet of total buildable space.

articles.title: Setting the Pace: A Day in the Life of North Bridge’s John Lewis
articles.narrative: This article provides a day-in-the-life profile of John Lewis, Vice President of Investments at North Bridge, a prominent C-PACE lender. It highlights his daily routine, including early morning workouts, family responsibilities, and a series of client and internal meetings in New York City. The narrative emphasizes the importance of relationship building in commercial real estate finance, particularly in the niche C-PACE lending sector, and the firm's efforts to educate developers and senior lenders on the benefits and mechanics of C-PACE financing.

articles.title: New York Developer Seeks to Build 296 Units in Flushing, Queens
articles.narrative: Developer Gary Chen has submitted a zoning change application to the New York City Department of City Planning for a proposed 10-story, mixed-use project in Flushing, Queens. The development would feature 296 residential units, with 75 designated as permanently affordable, alongside ground-floor commercial space and parking. Chen, who acquired the four parcels for the project in 2024 for $26.5 million, aims to transform an underutilized site into much-needed housing and employment opportunities in a transit-accessible area.

data_points.address: 32-02 Linden Place, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Proposed 10-story mixed-use project with 75 permanently affordable residential units, located just south of the Whitestone Expressway and a block from Leavitts Park.
data_points.project_notes: Developer acquired four existing properties for a combined $26.5 million in 2024 to assemble the site for this project.

articles.title: Berkadia Arranges $124.6M Loan for Refinancing of Irving Mixed-Use Property
articles.narrative: Berkadia has arranged a $124.6 million loan for the refinancing of Alesio Urban Center, a mixed-use property in Irving, TX. The property, owned by Harbor Group International, features 908 apartments and 55,499 square feet of commercial space. Blackstone provided the financing for the asset located in the Las Colinas district.

data_points.address: 385 Las Colinas Blvd E, Irving, TX
data_points.notable_features: Developed in phases between 1987 and 1995, the property includes 14 residential buildings, a structured parking garage, and 23 commercial spaces, with amenities such as five pools, a fitness center, business center, game lounge, running trail, and media/theater room.

articles.title: Triten Real Estate Delivers 400,000 SF Distribution Center in Humble, Texas
articles.narrative: Triten Real Estate Partners has completed Kenswick at 1960, a two-building, 400,000-square-foot distribution center in Humble, Texas. The project features 36-foot clear heights, office space, and extensive parking, including over five acres of outdoor storage. CBRE has been appointed as the leasing agent for the newly delivered industrial facility.

data_points.notable_features: Two-building distribution center with 36-foot clear heights, 9,000 square feet of office space, and over five acres of outdoor storage.

articles.title: NewForm Real Estate Completes 50,738 SF Adaptive Reuse Project Near Downtown Houston
articles.narrative: NewForm Real Estate has completed a 50,738-square-foot adaptive reuse project near downtown Houston. The project involved converting the historic Commercial National Bank Building, originally built in 1904, into a modern office facility with ground-floor retail and restaurant space. The restoration included extensive exterior work and upgraded lighting, preserving the building's historic character while modernizing its interior.

data_points.notable_features: Historic six-story building originally constructed in 1904, converted into modern office with retail/restaurant space.
data_points.project_notes: Adaptive reuse project involved exterior façade work, window repairs, masonry cleaning, and upgraded lighting.

articles.title: Spring Valley Construction Breaks Ground on 17,400 SF Retail Project in Flower Mound, Texas
articles.narrative: Spring Valley Construction Co. has commenced construction on the initial phase of a 17,400-square-foot retail development in Flower Mound, Texas. This retail component is part of the larger Silveron Park mixed-use project, which will ultimately include extensive office space and residential units. Weitzman is overseeing the development and leasing for the retail portion, while Thompson Realty Capital is managing the office and residential elements.

articles.title: Burlington Debuts 2 MSF Distribution Center in Ellabell, Georgia
articles.narrative: Burlington Stores Inc. has opened its first distribution center in Georgia, a 2 million-square-foot facility in Ellabell, Bryan County. This new center is expected to create 1,500 jobs at full capacity and includes extensive office space and employee amenities. The facility will support Burlington's expanding network of discount apparel stores, with the company planning to open approximately 115 net new stores nationwide by early 2027.

data_points.notable_features: Includes 40,000 square feet of office space with a full-service cafeteria, fitness center, locker room with showers, meditation room, and maternity room.

articles.title: High gas prices still putting a hurt on hotel industry
articles.narrative: The U.S. hospitality sector is currently navigating a challenging summer travel season marked by cautious consumer spending due to high energy costs, increased airfare, and softer employment conditions, according to a report by Marcus & Millichap. Despite these short-term pressures, the industry is poised for a brighter future, supported by a significant shortage of new hotel construction and improving investment activity. The report highlights that limited-service hotels are experiencing the greatest slowdown in demand, while select-service, upscale, and luxury properties are demonstrating more resilience. The constrained development pipeline, which remains 34% below 2019 levels, is preventing oversupply and bolstering hotel fundamentals, while investment activity has seen a 19% increase in transaction volume over the past year.
articles.market_intelligence: Consumer sentiment has fallen to historically low levels. Occupancy in the limited-service segment has trended downward since 2019, falling from over 58% before the pandemic to the low-54% range by late 2025. Total room nights sold in the select-service segment have remained relatively stable since 2023. Hotel transaction volume has recovered to levels consistent with 2016. Pricing has remained relatively stable since 2023. Delinquency rates are relatively modest.

articles.title: Glenstar acquires 500 W. Monroe office tower in Chicago
articles.narrative: Glenstar, in a joint venture with a private investor, has acquired the 966,924-square-foot, Class-A office tower at 500 W. Monroe in Chicago's West Loop through an all-cash purchase. The acquisition aligns with Glenstar's strategy of investing in high-quality, well-located buildings in the downtown market. Glenstar plans extensive renovations to modernize the lobby and common areas, introduce new amenities, and implement its tenant engagement program, Glenstar Connect. The firm will also assume asset and property management responsibilities for the 44-story building, which offers large, column-free floor plates and excellent connectivity to transit and major expressways.

data_points.notable_features: Class-A office tower with a 1,300-stall parking garage, 44 stories, virtually column-free floor plates, expansive windows, and seamless access to Union and Ogilvie train stations and major expressways.
data_points.project_notes: Acquired in an all-cash purchase. Glenstar will launch a hospitality-driven renovation including a full lobby transformation, common area upgrades, and new amenities. Glenstar Connect, an app-based tenant engagement program, will also be introduced.

articles.title: Lee & Associates closes 56,780-square-foot industrial lease in Grand Prairie
articles.narrative: Lee & Associates Dallas-Fort Worth has successfully brokered a new lease for a 56,780-square-foot industrial property in Grand Prairie, TX. The transaction involved an undisclosed tenant securing space at the Industrial Crossing development, with Lee & Associates representing the landlord.

data_points.address: 2401 W Marshall Dr, Grand Prairie, TX

articles.title: Rent control measure slashed from Massachusetts ballot
articles.narrative: The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court has blocked a statewide rent control ballot measure from appearing before voters in November, citing a religious exemption within the proposal as unconstitutional. This decision marks a victory for landlord-backed coalitions who argued against the measure, which would have allowed municipalities to cap annual rent increases at 5 percent. Opponents of rent control celebrated the ruling, while proponents indicated they might attempt to reintroduce the measure in the future. The proposed initiative, supported by housing advocates, faced criticism from industry groups and an economic analysis suggesting it could significantly reduce property values.

data_points.notable_features: Proposed rent control measure would have allowed cities and towns to cap annual rent increases at 5 percent, with carveouts for owner-occupied buildings with four or fewer units and new construction for the first 10 years.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Tufts University Center for State Policy Analysis

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: State Attorney General Andrea Campbell’s office

articles.title: Office Properties Income Trust emerges from bankruptcy
articles.narrative: Office Properties Income Trust (OPI) has successfully emerged from Chapter 11 bankruptcy, significantly reducing its debt by approximately $714 million and converting a substantial portion of its creditor base into new shareholders. The restructuring plan, approved by the court in April, reinstated OPI's $425 million revolving credit facility, $300 million in secured notes, and $177 million in mortgage debt. The company's existing shareholders were wiped out, with former noteholders, including affiliates of Helix Partners Management and Redwood Capital Management, now controlling a significant portion of the reorganized entity. OPI will continue to be managed by The RMR Group under new five-year agreements.

data_points.project_notes: The restructuring eliminated approximately $714 million of debt, reduced leverage, and converted creditors into shareholders. The company emerged with approximately $1.7 billion in debt and 22 million newly issued common shares outstanding. Existing shareholders were wiped out.

articles.financing: OPI reinstated its $425 million revolving credit facility (9.1% interest rate), $300 million in secured notes due 2029, and $177 million in mortgage debt. Holders of various secured and unsecured notes received a mix of newly issued debt, equity, warrants, and rights tied to a $35 million equity offering.

articles.title: Housing bill bounces back to House after Senate passage
articles.narrative: The Senate has passed the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act with an 85-5 vote, sending the housing affordability bill back to the House for a final vote this week. A key provision of the bill is a ban on large institutional investors from acquiring single-family homes, a measure that has been a point of contention but gained significant support. Notably, the legislation does not include a previously debated mandate that would have required build-to-rent investors to divest rental homes within seven years, a provision that had caused significant concern and deterred investment in the sector. The bill also features programs to aid communities in identifying housing opportunities, provides grants and loans for property rehabilitation, expands the definition of manufactured housing to potentially circumvent local zoning challenges, links federal grants to local housing construction, and streamlines environmental reviews for development projects.

articles.title: Housing Notes: Greenspan made the Fed famous
articles.narrative: This opinion piece reflects on the legacy of former Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan, who recently passed away at 100. The author argues that Greenspan's policy mix of low short-term interest rates, a hands-off regulatory approach, and public encouragement of complex mortgage products significantly fueled the 2000s housing boom, increased leverage, and exacerbated the subsequent bust. The author also critiques the over-obsession with interest rates as the primary gauge of monetary policy, a trend Greenspan helped cement, and suggests that the lack of regulatory oversight during his tenure continues to distort the U.S. housing market.

articles.title: NY Dirt: Can’t pay, won’t pay
articles.narrative: This article explores the reasons behind declining rent collections in New York's affordable housing sector, a trend that began during the pandemic and continues to impact operators' financial stability. While no single cause has been identified, theories include tenants facing increased economic hardship, a shift in tenant attitudes towards rent payments influenced by pandemic-era assistance and eviction moratoriums, and city policies that inadvertently incentivize tenants to fall deeply into arrears before receiving financial aid. The variability in collections across similar properties suggests complex social dynamics at play within the rental system.

articles.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending June 19, 2026
articles.narrative: This article summarizes the top construction permit applications filed in New York City during the week ending June 19, 2026, highlighting significant new building, major alteration, and demolition projects across various boroughs. Notable new residential developments include a 127-unit project in Crotona Park East and a 108-unit project in Kew Gardens. Major alteration permits were filed for an office expansion in Graniteville and a significant renovation in Midtown. The largest demolition permit was for a partial teardown on the Upper East Side to make way for a new condominium development.

articles.title: PolicyPro: Mamdani faces political referendum, housing shapes Assembly race
articles.narrative: Today's Democratic primaries in New York City are poised to significantly impact the real estate industry, testing the extent of the Democratic Party's leftward shift. The elections will gauge Mayor Zohran Mamdani's political influence, particularly through his endorsed candidates in high-stakes congressional races. A key Lower Manhattan Assembly race, replacing long-time incumbent Deborah Glick, is seen as a referendum on housing policy, pitting a candidate aligned with anti-development sentiment against a pro-growth advocate. The results will indicate whether the district remains resistant to new development or is open to candidates supporting increased housing construction.

articles.title: REBNY legislative lead resigns after tearing down political signs
articles.narrative: Dev Awasthi, REBNY's vice president of legislative affairs for New York, resigned after being filmed removing and trashing campaign signs for congressional candidate Jack Schlossberg. REBNY stated that Awasthi's actions were inappropriate and did not meet their employee standards, leading to his immediate termination. The incident occurred near Third Avenue and 32nd Street in Manhattan, within the 12th Congressional district.

people.name: Dev Awasthi
people.title: Vice President of Legislative Affairs for New York

people.name: Carl Hum
people.title: Senior Vice President and General Counsel

articles.title: Rosen roadblock? SL Green sues RFR over Madison Avenue building access
articles.narrative: SL Green is suing RFR Holding to gain access to adjacent properties at 350 Madison Avenue and 10 East 45th Street, which SL Green claims is necessary to proceed with the demolition and below-grade construction phases of its planned 850,000-square-foot office tower at 346 Madison Avenue. SL Green alleges RFR has repeatedly ignored requests for access, causing significant delays to the project. The REIT is seeking a court-ordered two-year license to enter portions of RFR's properties to complete the work.

data_points.address: 346 Madison Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Planned 46-story, all-electric office tower with column-free floorplates, nine terrace levels, and a two-story amenity center.
data_points.project_notes: SL Green previously acquired the site for $160 million in September 2025. In May 2026, SL Green sold a 49% stake in the project to Mori Building, valuing it at $175 million.

articles.title: Miami Design District brings another marquee mixed-use project
articles.narrative: Miami Design District Associates and Raycliff Capital are partnering to develop Sweetbird North, an eight-story, 120,500-square-foot mixed-use project in the Miami Design District. The development will feature over 92,000 square feet of office space targeting creative industries and 22,500 square feet of ground-floor and second-story retail. Construction is slated to begin in August 2026 and conclude in early 2028, with the design by globally recognized architecture firm Snøhetta.

data_points.notable_features: Features a layered facade of mesh screens and planted terraces; office space targets creative industries.
data_points.project_notes: Developed on a 17,000-square-foot site.

articles.market_intelligence: Developers in the Design District are relying on Miami's continued influx of companies and executives to drive demand for new office space, offering an alternative to established business hubs like downtown, Brickell, and Wynwood. Nearly 1,000 residential units are planned in the neighborhood.

articles.title: Will Miami commissioners put Live Local Act in legal crosshairs?
articles.narrative: A Miami City Commissioner has sponsored a measure to challenge Florida's Live Local Act, a state law designed to incentivize affordable housing development through tax breaks and zoning exemptions. The commissioner argues that while housing initiatives are welcome, the city must protect residents from development pressures and policies that override local planning and undermine neighborhood character. This move aligns Miami with other localities, such as Hollywood and Hillsborough County, that have initiated legal action against the controversial law, which opponents claim erodes local control.

articles.title: Stonerock loses downtown office buildings in $66M foreclosure
articles.narrative: Two downtown Miami office buildings, previously acquired by Stonerock Capital in 2022 with a mortgage from Ardent Companies, are now facing foreclosure. The foreclosure judgment, totaling $65.7 million, was issued after Stonerock Capital allegedly defaulted on loan payments and failed to repay the mortgage upon maturity, as well as neglecting property tax payments. The properties are scheduled for an online auction in July.

data_points.notable_features: The 200 Southeast First Street building includes a data center and ground-floor retail. The 44 West Flagler Street building is anchored by a bank.
data_points.project_notes: The original mortgage of $58.3 million was provided by Ardent Companies in 2022 for Stonerock Capital's acquisition of the buildings for $56.7 million. Triple Double Real Estate was a partner in the initial acquisition but left the venture in 2023. The foreclosure complaint was filed in 2025, alleging default on a $49.4 million mortgage and missed property tax payments for 2023.

articles.market_intelligence: Miami office leasing reached 918,000 square feet in the first quarter, with the tech industry accounting for 37% and banking, finance, insurance, and real estate accounting for 29%. Downtown Miami had a 19% direct vacancy, which is among the highest of the city's submarkets.

articles.title: Blue Road, Ilia cut hotel rooms, win resi upsize for North Miami Beach project
articles.narrative: Blue Road and Ilia Development Group received approval from North Miami Beach officials to increase the residential unit count for their condo tower, The William, from 344 to 374 units, while eliminating 30 planned hotel rooms. The 25-story project, located at 2040 Northeast 163rd Street, will also include 4,500 square feet of retail and is slated to begin construction by early next year. The developers have already presold 23 percent of the units, indicating strong market interest in the area's evolving landscape.

data_points.address: 2040 Northeast 163rd Street, North Miami Beach, FL
data_points.original_plan: 23-story building with 334 residential units, 30 hotel rooms, 7,300 square feet of retail and 410 parking spaces
data_points.notable_features: Features a pickleball court on the 25th-floor roof deck, a ninth-floor amenity deck with a pool, club room, office areas, and a gym.
data_points.project_notes: The 1.1-acre property was previously acquired for $7.5 million in November 2022. The development replaces a two-story office building completed in 1968. Presales prices for condo units ranged between $380,000 and $1 million at launch in September 2026.

articles.title: Hostage to the HOA: How some developers keep a tight grip on owner associations
articles.narrative: This article examines how some residential developers in Florida are allegedly retaining control of homeowners' associations (HOAs) and condominium associations long after state law dictates they should cede power to residents. The practice, which experts say goes against the spirit of the law, allows developers to maintain contractor relationships, delay construction defect lawsuits, and potentially profit from in-house management contracts. Homeowners in several complexes, including Portofino Vista in St. Cloud and Brickell Key in Miami, claim developer-controlled boards neglect property upkeep, lack financial transparency, and levy significant assessments for projects that may primarily benefit the developer's future plans. While developers often assert they are operating within their rights, critics argue this prolonged control grants immense power, enabling them to assess fees and pursue legal action against homeowners who resist.

articles.title: Skokie home breaks sale record with $2M-plus closing
articles.narrative: A single-family home in Skokie, IL, sold for $2.25 million, setting a new record for the Chicago suburb. The property, built in 2006, features a home theater, gym, and a backyard opening onto the Evanston Golf Club. The sale price of $401 per square foot reflects the custom-built nature of the residence.

data_points.notable_features: Features a three-car garage, home theater, gym, and direct access to Evanston Golf Club amenities including a golf course, tennis courts, and pool facility.

articles.market_intelligence: Only three percent of the 699 homes sold in Skokie in the past year exceeded $1 million.

articles.title: Glenstar, Halloran complete bargain purchase of 46-story Loop office tower for under $100M
articles.narrative: A joint venture between Glenstar and Patrick Halloran has acquired the 46-story office tower at 500 West Monroe Street in downtown Chicago for less than $100 million, a significant discount from its previous sale price. The buyers plan to invest in renovations and new amenities to boost leasing momentum, as the building was approximately 75% leased last year and faces a major lease expiration in 2027. The previous owner, Spear Street Capital, surrendered the property via deed-in-lieu of foreclosure after defaulting on a $270 million loan.

data_points.notable_features: 46-story office tower.
data_points.project_notes: The distressed $270 million loan on the building was acquired for less than $100 million. Spear Street Capital previously bought the building for $412 million in October 2019.

articles.market_intelligence: Downtown office market has seen weakened demand overall due to elevated interest rates and hybrid work schedules, impacting office space values outside of trophy assets.

articles.title: Chicago sues Airbnb and host who allegedly violated short-term rental ordinance hundreds of times
articles.narrative: The city of Chicago has filed a lawsuit against Airbnb and a prominent host, Milan Rubenstein, alleging widespread violations of its short-term rental ordinance. The city claims Rubenstein illegally operated over 200 unregistered units between 2024 and 2025, using a single hotel license for properties across the city instead of individual registrations. The lawsuit also accuses Airbnb of prioritizing profit over compliance by failing to remove non-compliant listings, providing incomplete data, and refusing to utilize the city's data platform to verify registrations. Chicago is seeking the surrender of profits from the illegally rented units and daily fines for both parties, aiming to establish a precedent for better enforcement of its 2016 ordinance designed to regulate the short-term rental market and address housing and safety concerns.

data_points.project_notes: Milan Rubenstein previously acquired parcels for a 344-unit Mount Prospect apartment complex between 2010 and 2014 for $24.4 million and sold it in 2014 for $33 million.

articles.title: Chicago affordable housing giant Peter Holsten to retire, sell portfolio and firm
articles.narrative: Peter Holsten, founder of Holsten Real Estate Development Corporation, is retiring and selling his Chicago portfolio of 17 affordable housing buildings, along with the development firm and its management arm. The portfolio includes 2,638 apartment units, with 758 designated as Chicago Housing Authority mixed-income units, and over two million square feet of commercial and residential space. The sale is being marketed by Walker and Dunlop, with the firm's five decades of experience in affordable housing cited as a key draw for potential buyers.

data_points.project_notes: The portfolio includes 17 buildings, with 758 units designated as Chicago Housing Authority mixed-income units.

articles.title: Austin lands another AI tenant as Partly moves HQ from New Zealand
articles.narrative: Partly, an AI company specializing in the automotive sector, is relocating its headquarters from New Zealand to Austin, TX. The company plans to significantly expand its workforce in the coming years and is actively seeking 20,000 to 30,000 square feet of downtown office space. The move is supported by a recent $50 million Series B funding round and is driven by Austin's proximity to "real world businesses" and a strong talent pool, including graduates from the University of Texas and Bay Area transplants.

data_points.notable_features: Company is seeking 20,000-30,000 SF of open-concept office space downtown.
data_points.project_notes: Partly recently raised $50 million in Series B funding, which will be used to scale its U.S. workforce.

articles.market_intelligence: While the influx of tech companies to Texas has slowed, AI-based companies may be an exception, bolstered by the presence of tech giants like Google and Nvidia. Austin's office market had 1.7 million square feet of space planned or under construction at the beginning of the year, with tech companies accounting for one-third of pre-leased projects.

articles.title: Frisco mansion with unique 3D-printed steel frame lists for $15M
articles.narrative: A luxury mansion in Frisco, TX, featuring a digitally designed and 3D-printed steel frame, has been listed for $15 million. The innovative construction method significantly reduced framing time. The 13,559-square-foot residence, expected to be completed in 2027, offers six bedrooms, six full bathrooms, a glass elevator, and an infinity-edge pool, with views of The Star and a nature preserve. The developer noted the steel frame provides enhanced wind resistance, a valuable feature in the region.

data_points.notable_features: Features a digitally designed and 3D-printed steel framework, a glass elevator, and an infinity edge pool, with views of The Star and a nature preserve.
data_points.project_notes: The 3D-printed steel frame reduced framing time from 3-6 months to 4 weeks. The property is located in the gated Hills of Kingswood community.

articles.title: HGI scores $124M refi deal for one of Irving’s largest apartment complexes
articles.narrative: Harbor Group International has secured a $124.7 million refinancing loan for Alesio Urban Center, a large apartment complex in Irving, TX. The financing, provided by Blackstone, will be used to retire the existing mortgage and cover closing costs for the 908-unit property. This marks the third financing event for the property since Harbor Group International acquired it in 2021, demonstrating ongoing capital management for the asset.

data_points.address: 385 East Las Colinas Boulevard, Irving, TX
data_points.notable_features: Property includes 14 residential buildings and 23 commercial buildings with retail space, five swimming pools, a gym, yoga studio, conference facilities, and a running trail.
data_points.project_notes: Property was built in 1987 and 1995. Harbor Group International previously acquired the property in 2021 with a $117 million loan and refinanced it in 2023 with a $122 million fixed-rate loan while conducting $4.5 million in renovations.

articles.title: Johnson Development lines up first phase of $5B Denton master-planned community
articles.narrative: Johnson Development Services is set to break ground on the initial phase of Cole Ranch, a 3,169-acre master-planned community near Denton, Texas. This $5 billion development will introduce 4,365 single-family homes, a 55-acre business park, 156 acres of commercial space, and 200 acres for new schools. The first phase will focus on infrastructure and model homes, with home sales projected to commence in late 2027, addressing Denton's significant population growth.

data_points.phase_1: Infrastructure and model homes, with home sales anticipated in late 2027
data_points.notable_features: Includes 1,200 acres of open space and recreation features, a 55-acre business park, 156 acres of commercial development, and 200 acres for schools within Denton Independent School District.
data_points.project_notes: The site has been owned by the Cole family since the 1930s. Planning for the project spanned over a decade, with initial public hearings in the mid-2010s and a municipal management district approved in 2019.

articles.market_intelligence: Denton's population increased by 21 percent from 2020 to 2025.

articles.title: Heitman seizes downtown Oakland office building from KKR, TMG
articles.narrative: Heitman Capital Management has foreclosed on a $239.6 million loan, seizing the 535,000-square-foot office building at 1221 Broadway in downtown Oakland from previous owners KKR and TMG Partners. TMG Partners, a former co-owner, will remain as the operating partner for the 24-story tower, with plans to renovate the property and attract new tenants. The foreclosure highlights the ongoing challenges in Oakland's office market, where rising vacancies and declining property values have led to several landlords defaulting on their loans.

data_points.notable_features: Glass-covered trophy tower designed by Cesar Pelli, includes ground-floor shops and restaurants at City Center.
data_points.project_notes: KKR and TMG Partners acquired the building in 2019 for $255.34 million. Clorox's anchor lease for half the building expires next year.

articles.market_intelligence: Oakland office vacancies have risen and property values have plummeted since the pandemic, leading to numerous foreclosures and properties being sold at distressed prices. Last month, the 206,000-square-foot Edgewater Park Plaza office park went into foreclosure. In January, Deutsche Bank seized three office buildings from Starwood Capital at 2100 Franklin Street, 2101 Webster Street, and 1901 Harrison Street.

articles.title: Beverly Hills nonprofit snags 262-unit apartment complex in San Jose for $88M
articles.narrative: The Bedford Affordable Housing Foundation, a newly formed Beverly Hills-based nonprofit, has acquired the 262-unit Almaden Terrace apartments in San Jose for $87.7 million. The transaction was financed by Fannie Mae and the California Statewide Communities Development Authority, which issued $71.4 million in revenue bonds. The acquisition includes affordability covenants, requiring at least 40% of units to be reserved for low-income residents and 75% for those earning up to 80% of the area median income, addressing the rising rental costs in the San Jose market.

data_points.address: 2118 Canoas Garden Avenue, San Jose, CA
data_points.notable_features: Two-story complex with a pool, media center, movie theater, picnic area, and dog park.
data_points.project_notes: At least 40% of units (105 apartments) are set aside for low-income residents, and at least 75% of units (197 apartments) are for residents at 80% of the area median income.

articles.market_intelligence: Average asking rents in San Jose have climbed above $3,100 per month and are projected to rise faster than any other major U.S. city. The area median income for one person in Santa Clara County is $115,080 per year.

articles.title: Congress Passes ROAD to Housing Act; President Expected to Sign into Law
articles.narrative: The U.S. Congress has passed the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, which is expected to be signed into law by the President. The legislation includes a provision to increase banks' public welfare investment (PWI) cap from 15% to 20%, aiming to boost private capital for affordable housing development. While it restricts institutional ownership of single-family homes, build-to-rent housing is exempt, and existing institutional holdings are not required to be sold. The Act also features reforms to modernize federal housing programs, reduce development barriers, and expand housing supply and affordability nationwide.

articles.title: Trident Capital Group Expands Industrial Portfolio by 2 M SF in Ohio
articles.narrative: Trident Capital Group has significantly expanded its industrial portfolio in the Columbus, Ohio region by adding approximately 1.94 million square feet through a combination of new developments and acquisitions. The expansion includes a recently delivered and fully leased industrial facility, a second phase currently under construction, and a distribution facility acquired in April that is also fully leased. Additionally, Trident Capital Group, in partnership with O'Connor Capital Partners, is developing two large speculative industrial centers, one of which is nearing completion.

data_points.project_notes: This article describes multiple transactions and developments by Trident Capital Group, including the acquisition of a 308,358 SF distribution facility in April 2026, the delivery of a 191,077 SF industrial facility in 2025, the ongoing construction of a 177,607 SF industrial facility, and the development of two industrial centers totaling 1,262,410 SF.

articles.title: Moinian Group Secures Quartet of Leases in Midtown South
articles.narrative: The Moinian Group has secured four new leases totaling 70,000 square feet at 60 Madison Avenue in Midtown South. The transactions underscore the building's appeal to innovative and expanding companies seeking premium office space in a vibrant Manhattan business district, according to the landlord.

data_points.address: 60 Madison Ave., New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Located directly on Madison Square Park.

articles.title: Cityview, Stockbridge Cut Ribbon on Gardena Multifamily
articles.narrative: Cityview and Stockbridge have celebrated the grand opening of Apollo, a 265-unit multifamily community in Los Angeles' South Bay. The project aims to address the region's housing shortage by providing new residences in a job-rich area, specifically within the aerospace corridor. Its strategic location offers convenient access to major employment centers, public transit, and key regional attractions.

data_points.notable_features: Located in the heart of the aerospace corridor, within five miles of over 260,000 jobs, and a short walk from the Metro Green Line.

articles.title: Jonathan Mechanic Elected as Next Chair of REBNY
articles.narrative: Jonathan L. Mechanic, Chairman of Fried Frank's Real Estate Department, has been elected as the next Chair of the Real Estate Board of New York (REBNY). He will succeed Jed Walentas, Chairman and CEO of Two Trees Management, and will begin his three-year term on January 1, 2027. Mechanic expressed his honor in continuing REBNY's work to strengthen the economy, create housing, and support New York City's growth.

companies_people.label: Association / Leadership News
companies_people.firm_name: Real Estate Board of New York (REBNY)

articles.title: Vacancy Has Peaked For Life Sciences. The Good News Ends There
articles.narrative: While lab leasing shows modest improvement in select U.S. markets, overall conditions for life sciences landlords remain challenging due to declining rents and increasing concessions. Availability has contracted by approximately 2M SF in the top 12 lab markets over the past nine months, but this has come at the cost of an 18% rent decrease since 2023 and longer free rent periods. The market is seeing a shift where non-life sciences tenants are increasingly absorbing lab space, and developers are pivoting properties to attract AI, robotics, and advanced manufacturing users, particularly in the Bay Area. Despite some positive signs in biotech funding and stock performance, the real estate sector faces continued tenant-favorable conditions and uncertainty from past federal research funding cuts.

people.name: Travis McCready

articles.title: Amazon Turns Buyer As Logistics Market Throws Up Opportunities
articles.narrative: Amazon is actively seeking to acquire and lease logistics and industrial properties across Europe, particularly in the UK and Germany, capitalizing on current market uncertainties to secure long-term assets. The e-commerce giant prioritizes proximity to major population centers, access to labor, and increasingly, access to power for its automated facilities and electric vehicle fleets. This strategic shift towards property ownership reflects Amazon's long-term investment approach in a market where core capital is largely hesitant, creating opportunities for well-capitalized players.

people.title: Head of Global Strategic Properties Northern Europe

people.name: Thomas Blangy

articles.market_intelligence: UK industrial investment reached a three-year high of £10B in 2025, but Q1 2026 volumes were £1.9B, a 15% decrease year-over-year. Industrial yields have risen 25 basis points to approximately 5%. There is a significant gap between prime and secondary yields, and a lack of new development in the sector over the past five years continues to support rental growth.

articles.title: Virginia Budget Keeps Data Center Tax Breaks, Adds $600M Energy Levy
articles.narrative: Virginia lawmakers have passed a $250 billion state budget that retains the sales and use tax exemption for data centers, a key incentive credited with establishing the state as a global data center hub. However, the new budget introduces a significant levy on data center energy consumption, projected to generate up to $600 million annually. This compromise legislation, which avoids a government shutdown, also mandates environmental studies and noise abatement regulations for data centers, signaling a shift towards balancing industry growth with environmental and community impacts. Analysts suggest this new energy tax, while not existential for large operators, could increase electricity costs by approximately 11% for data centers in the state and may foreshadow similar regulatory trends in other markets.
articles.market_intelligence: Virginia has the world's densest concentration of data center facilities. The state was the first to implement sales and use tax exemptions to promote data center development. The combination of rising fiscal pressures and anxiety about the socioeconomic fallout from AI adoption is likely to make data centers a ripe target for new revenue proposals in other markets.

articles.title: U.S. Ranks First In Being 'Zillow For Money Launderers'
articles.narrative: A new report from the Tax Justice Network identifies the U.S. as the leading country globally for real estate money laundering, earning it the moniker "Zillow for money launderers." The Financial Secrecy Index, which ranks 141 countries, assesses complicity in hiding illicit funds through real estate based on factors like public access to ownership information, government access to centralized records, and requirements for beneficial ownership disclosure from foreign entities. The U.S. scored highest on this index, followed by Switzerland, Singapore, Hong Kong, and the UAE. Between 2015 and 2020, an estimated $2.3 billion was laundered through U.S. real estate, with 30% in commercial properties, largely due to fewer due diligence and reporting regulations compared to financial accounts. While the Biden administration attempted to tighten controls, recent actions under the Trump administration, such as halting enforcement of the Corporate Transparency Act and a federal judge blocking a rule requiring disclosure for all-cash residential deals, have weakened these efforts. However, broader anti-fraud measures by Freddie Mac and Fannie Mae in 2024, including enhanced financial reporting and KYC requirements for multifamily transactions, could indirectly make money laundering more challenging.
